nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving to near-still blackwater streams, flooded forest margins, and shallow tributaries with dense leaf litter, submerged wood, and aquatic vegetation; very soft, highly acidic, tannin-stained water with minimal mineral content

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java moss, Riccia, floating plants)
driftwood and twisted roots for tannins
dark substrate to enhance color and reduce stress
subdued lighting or floating plants to diffuse light

diet.notes: Micropredator with a small mouth that cannot take standard-sized flake whole. Best fed micro-sized live and frozen foods: baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), Moina, Daphnia, grindal worms, and micro-worms. Will accept high-quality micro-pellets and finely crushed flake that float at the surface. Variety is important; a diet of only dry food leads to slow decline.

goodWith: other small peaceful characids (e.g., ember tetras, chili rasboras)
dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma spp.) — pencilfish serve as excellent dither fish
other Nannostomus species of similar size
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving fish that outcompete at feeding
cherry shrimp and crystal shrimp (small fry and juveniles may be predated)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Nannostomus marginatus. Verify license before use.

summary: The smallest of the common pencilfish (~2–3.5 cm), the Dwarf Pencilfish is a blackwater specialist from Amazonian soft-acid streams. It displays a bold horizontal red-and-black stripe pattern with red below the central band, fading to an oblique cryptic pattern at night. Best kept in soft, tannin-rich water in groups of 8+.
careNotes: Nannostomus marginatus is the smallest pencilfish regularly seen in the hobby, reaching only 2–3.5 cm in length. It is a strict blackwater specialist: wild fish inhabit tannin-stained, near-stagnant forest streams with pH typically below 6 and hardness close to zero. In the aquarium, soft acidic water (pH 4.0–7.0, gH 0–10, kH 0–4) is strongly preferred; fish kept long-term in hard alkaline tap water often show faded coloration, reduced lifespan, and poor breeding success. Using reverse osmosis water remineralised to very low hardness, or adding Indian almond (catappa) leaves and driftwood to soften and acidify, is recommended. A tank of at least 40 L with a 60 cm footprint suits a group of 8–10 fish. Filtration should provide minimal surface agitation — a sponge filter or a spray-bar directed toward the glass is ideal. Dense planting (Java moss, Riccia fluitans, floating Salvinia or Pistia) and a leaf-litter layer are strongly encouraged. Because of its tiny mouth, all food must be micro-sized: Artemia nauplii, Moina, Daphnia, micro-pellets, and finely crushed flake. Standard-sized flake is too large to swallow. Keep in groups of 8 or more; larger numbers reduce male rivalry and encourage bolder, more visible behaviour. N. marginatus exhibits a nocturnal colour change, assuming paler oblique bars rather than the daytime horizontal stripes — a normal behaviour, not disease. It is frequently confused with N. trifasciatus (three-lined pencilfish), which is larger and lacks the red flush below the central stripe that characterises N. marginatus. See also: [N. trifasciatus] (three-lined pencilfish, similar but larger); [N. mortenthaleri] (coral red pencilfish, brighter red coloration).
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is achievable but uncommon without deliberate setup. A dedicated breeding tank of 10–15 L with extremely soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.0, hardness 1–3 dGH, temperature 27–29°C) and a Java moss mat or fine-leaved spawning mop is recommended. Eggs are scattered among plants; adults will eat eggs if not removed. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours; fry become free-swimming within 4–5 days and require infusoria or similar microscopic foods initially, graduating to micro-worms and Artemia nauplii. Males can be identified by a slimmer body profile; one form from the Rio Negro also shows thickened anal-fin rays in males.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nannostomus marginatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (20–25 mm SL), temperature (22–28°C), pH (4.0–7.0), hardness (18–179 ppm ≈ 1–10 dGH), native range (Guyana, Suriname, Colombia, Peru, Ecuador, Brazil), blackwater biotope, micropredator diet requirements, minimum tank base 45×30 cm (~41 L), grouping advice (10+), nocturnal colour change behaviour, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Nannostomus marginatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (3.5 cm TL), temperature (24–26°C), pH (5.8–7.5), hardness (up to 15 dH), taxonomy (Lebiasinidae, Characiformes), native range (Colombia, Guyana, Peru, Suriname, Venezuela, lower–middle Amazon). FishBase lists max TL 3.5 cm; used 3.5 cm as max per FishBase morphological maximum.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pencilfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General pencilfish care guide covering N. marginatus alongside congeners. Confirmed: temperature 75–80°F (24–27°C), pH 6.5–7.5, minimum 10-gallon (38 L) tank, micro-sized floating foods (baby brine shrimp, cyclops, daphnia, crushed flake), minimum group of 6, compatibility with corydoras/tetras/rasboras/dwarf cichlids, incompatibility with cherry and crystal shrimp.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Colorful Pencils for the Freshwater Aquarium
additional.notes: Species-specific data for N. marginatus: size 2.5–5 cm, lifespan 5–6 years, temperature 23–26°C, pH 6.5–7.0, hardness below 10 dGH, minimum 50 L / 60 cm tank for 10 fish, live foods (Artemia, Daphnia, fruit flies, tubifex), breeding setup (pH 5.5–6, 1–3 dGH, 29°C, Java moss). Lifespan estimate (5–6 yr) used as maximum.

fish.breedingNotes: Requires a dedicated soft-acid breeding tank (pH 5.5–6, 1–3 dGH, 27–29°C) with Java moss or fine spawning mop. Eggs scattered; adults eat eggs so parents should be removed. Fry need infusoria initially. Rarely bred without deliberate setup.
